# Version Retrospective ‚Äì v1.6.0 ‚Äî Advisor Tool API Catch-up (Opus 4.7)

## Version Summary

v1.6.0 caught the playground up to Anthropic's April 2026 advisor tool documentation changes: Claude Opus 4.7 replaces Opus 4.6 as the only documented advisor, Opus 4.7 is added as a new executor option, a new `xhigh` effort level is available on Opus 4.7, and advisor-side caching carries a new "keep consistent mid-conversation" guidance. The Anthropic evaluator judge was upgraded to Opus 4.7. During pricing verification, an incorrect Opus 4.6 price (still at $15/$75 per MTok in our code) surfaced ‚Äî Anthropic had dropped Opus-tier pricing to $5/$25 at some point and our trace-pane cost estimates had been inflated 3√ó for Opus 4.6 conversations. That was corrected in the same version. Six features (F36‚ÄìF40, plus the price correction bundled into F36) shipped.

## What Went Well

- **`/check-advisor-tool-updates` triggered the whole version.** The command surfaced three clear doc-side changes (advisor model, new executor, `xhigh`, caching guidance) with pinpoint citations, and the scope flowed naturally from that. The diff-driven workflow keeps catch-up versions small and focused instead of accreting drift.
- **Pricing verification caught a stale Opus 4.6 price.** The user's instinct that "Opus 4.6 has always been $5/$25" was right ‚Äî our code had been wrong for a while. Fetching Anthropic's authoritative pricing page during the pricing lookup task is what surfaced it. Without that verification step, we'd have shipped Opus 4.7 at accurate pricing while leaving the 4.6 entry stale.
- **Strengthened the catch-up command as a result.** The pricing URL and a "Model pricing" diff-checklist item are now part of `.claude/commands/check-advisor-tool-updates.md`. Every future run will surface pricing drift the same way it surfaces API-surface drift.
- **xhigh integrated cleanly with the existing Haiku-exclusion pattern.** The `ensureNaOption` / `removeNaOption` helpers for Haiku already existed; mirroring them as `ensureXhighOption` / `removeXhighOption` made the new feature feel native rather than grafted-on.
- **Phase 2 caught an `applySettings` edge case before it shipped.** Following the read of `applySettings` I noticed that setting `effortEl.value = "xhigh"` on page load would silently fail when xhigh wasn't in the DOM yet (because `updateEffortAvailability` runs later). Patched with an `ensureXhighOption()` call inside `applySettings` rather than relying on the restore path.
- **No Code View changes needed.** Code View reads live state and passes effort/model values through `JSON.stringify`. `xhigh` and `claude-opus-4-7` flowed through with zero code changes ‚Äî the design-doc verification in Phase 2 saved a tangent.
- **Caching lock respected the modal boundary.** The advisor-caching select lives in the Settings modal (not the Config panel), so the config-row `.locked` CSS class pattern didn't apply cleanly. Decision to use `disabled` + tooltip only (skip visual-parity CSS) matched user intuition and kept the change small.

## What Could Have Gone Better

- **First pricing fetch was ambiguous.** The `claude.com/pricing` summary returned by WebFetch matched Opus 4.6 and 4.7 but the layout made it easy to read as "Opus 4.6 dropped to $5/$25" when it had already been that price for a while. A second fetch of the structured docs page confirmed it, but the first result created a minute of "is this right?" friction. Future: go straight to the `platform.claude.com/docs/en/about-claude/pricing` table, which has proper rows per model.
- **`xhigh` as an HTML-native option vs JS-inserted was flip-flopped mid-build.** Added it to the HTML select first, then reverted and moved to JS-managed insertion once I realized it created a flash-of-wrong-content on initial load with the default Sonnet executor. Would have been cleaner to commit to the JS pattern from the design doc; the fix was cheap but it's a sign the design-doc review missed that detail.
- **The stale Opus 4.6 price is embarrassing in hindsight.** Opus-tier pricing dropped at some point between v1.4.0 and now, and no catch-up run surfaced it because the command only diffed the advisor tool docs, not the pricing page. This is exactly the class of drift the reference doc is supposed to catch. Addressed by F40 (pricing URL + snapshot + command update) but it shouldn't have taken a live user to notice.
- **README "rough per-eval cost" numbers are now probably stale too.** Updated them roughly in this version but they're still rough estimates based on the new $5/$25 rate. A proper cost calibration pass (run a real eval, capture the actual dollars, update README) would be more honest than eyeballed ranges.
- **Design doc asserted wrong browser behavior; caught by user in prod, not in testing.** Section 4.1 of the design doc claimed that when `applySettings` sets `advisorEl.value = "claude-opus-4-6"` against a dropdown where that option no longer exists, "the browser falls back to the remaining option (4.7)." That's wrong ‚Äî browsers clear the `<select>`'s value to empty string. Any user with persisted v1.5.0 settings saw a blank advisor dropdown on first load of v1.6.0. Fixed post-ship with a `setSelectIfValid()` helper in `applySettings` that no-ops when the saved value isn't in the DOM, letting the HTML `selected` default take over. Applied defensively to executor, advisor, mode, caching, and effort selects. Next user-driven save in the Settings modal self-heals the stale localStorage value.
- **`savedEffortValue` had multiple writers with overlapping semantics ‚Äî round-trip behavior broke.** Phase 2 testing revealed that switching Opus 4.7 ‚Üí Sonnet ‚Üí Opus 4.7 didn't restore xHigh; instead the dropdown stayed at High or jumped to Low. Two bugs. (1) The restore path in `updateEffortAvailability` only fired when `effortEl.value === "na"` ‚Äî fine for the Haiku round-trip but the xhigh demotion set `effortEl.value = "high"` (not "na"), so the restore was silently skipped on switch-back. (2) The Haiku branch overwrote `savedEffortValue = effortEl.value` after the xhigh-demotion branch had just written `"xhigh"` to it ‚Äî going through Haiku erased the xhigh intent entirely. Fix: decoupled `savedEffortValue` from `updateEffortAvailability` entirely. It's now written from exactly one place ‚Äî a dedicated `change` handler on `effortEl` that captures the user's last explicit selection. `updateEffortAvailability` reads it as truth and applies demotion per-executor. Also added a patch-save at the end of `updateEffortAvailability` because the bulk auto-save listener fires BEFORE `updateEffortAvailability` on executor change, so it was persisting the pre-demotion/pre-restore effort value and page-refresh would resurrect the stale value. Lesson: when state has both user-authored and system-demoted write paths, use separate storage (or a single-writer handler plus read-only consumers). Don't overload one variable with conflicting intents.
- **Phase 6 fired before USER testing of Phases 1-5.** The user asked to "do all phases at once, then test each phase after that" ‚Äî I read that as "implement everything, then test everything," and so ran Phase 6 (version bump, release notes, retrospective, feature-backlog completed marker) before the user had tested any of Phases 1-5. That's a real problem: release notes and retrospectives pre-commit to "done" statements while the code may still have undiscovered issues ‚Äî exactly what happened with the blank-advisor bug, which the retrospective and release notes both claimed was handled cleanly when it wasn't. The Cody workflow pattern is test-each-phase, commit-each-phase, then finalize. Rollback from a partially-committed "all phases implemented" state is harder than rollback from one-phase-at-a-time. Future: default to phase-by-phase handoff unless the user explicitly says "ship everything including finalize." When the request is ambiguous, ask.

## Lessons Learned

- **Diff the pricing page on every catch-up.** API surface and pricing drift independently and at different rates. Now codified as F40.
- **When a user's intuition contradicts code state, check the authoritative source immediately.** "We've always had that price for Opus 4.6" was correct; our code was wrong. Deferring to the code over the user's memory would have been the wrong call.
- **JS-managed option insertion for conditional dropdown options.** HTML-native options with CSS `display: none` would also work, but the `ensureNaOption`/`removeNaOption` pattern in this codebase is well-understood; following it kept the change consistent and avoided the flash-of-wrong-content problem.
- **Settings-modal lock doesn't need config-row visual parity.** The Settings modal is on-demand; native `disabled` grey-out plus tooltip is enough. Don't copy patterns that only make sense for always-visible config.
- **`applySettings` runs before `updateEffortAvailability` on init.** This ordering trap could bite future features that add conditional dropdown options too. Documenting it inline where both functions are defined would help future maintainers.
- **Never assume `<select>.value = "‚Ä¶"` falls back to the HTML default.** Modern browsers clear the selection to empty string instead. Whenever we drop or rename an option across versions, guard the set with `setSelectIfValid` or the persisted-state path will render blank. Design docs for any future dropdown-option removal should spell out the migration explicitly ‚Äî not assume graceful fallback.
- **Single-writer discipline for state variables.** When a single variable is written from multiple branches (Haiku save, xhigh-demotion save, init), writes race and overlap. Use a dedicated change event handler as the sole writer, or split into separate variables with narrower semantics. Also: when a programmatic side-effect changes a value that also has an auto-save listener, the listener fires on the old value (since it was wired to the input's change event, which in this case is the executor's, not the effort's). Either route through a single save-after-update pattern or explicitly patch-save after the side-effect.
- **"Do all phases at once" does not mean "also do Phase 6."** Phase 6 is the finalization phase ‚Äî version bumps, release notes, retrospective, marking backlog entries done. Those statements are assertions about shipped work and should only fire after the USER has verified the AGENT work. When a request is ambiguous about whether finalization is included, ask.

## Action Items

1. **Run the pricing-diff part of the catch-up command out of cycle.** The new `Step 3.4` (pricing WebFetch) only fires on full catch-up runs. If Anthropic changes pricing between API-surface releases, we'll miss it until the next catch-up. Consider a separate, cheaper "pricing only" check that runs more often ‚Äî or a scheduled version of `/check-advisor-tool-updates` that fires weekly.
2. **Add an end-to-end smoke harness for Code View snippets.** Still flagged in plan.md risks section: v1.5.0's TS/Python snippets were structurally validated but not executed. With Opus 4.7 + new tokenizer, a generated snippet silently failing on a field name change would not be caught by our current process.
3. **Calibrate README per-eval cost estimates.** Replace eyeballed ranges with actual measured dollars from a real eval run at each model's current pricing. Would take 10 minutes and tighten user expectations.
4. **Document the "xHigh requires bigger `max_tokens`" guidance more prominently.** Release notes mention it; the Settings modal `max-uses` / `max-tokens` inputs don't. Consider a contextual hint: when executor=Opus 4.7 AND effort=xhigh, show a subtle nudge under `max_tokens` if the value is under 32768.
5. **Watch for Opus 4.6-as-advisor deprecation date.** Empirically it still works, but Anthropic may cut it off without warning. If we see a grace-period deprecation note, consider adding a migration banner for users who have persisted 4.6 as the advisor (though that's already silently collapsed to 4.7 on first render in v1.6.0 because we removed the 4.6 option).
6. **Default to phase-by-phase handoff in `:cody build` unless finalization is explicitly requested.** When the user says "do all phases at once," implement the AGENT code/docs tasks for Phases 1 through N-1 (where N is the finalization phase), then stop and wait for USER testing before running Phase N. This matches the Cody workflow's intent and prevents shipping "done" assertions before the user has confirmed the code works.
7. **Audit other dropdowns for persisted-state fragility.** `applySettings` now guards five selects with `setSelectIfValid`, but any future dropdown where option values can be removed across versions needs the same treatment. Candidates to watch: the system-prompt-preset dropdown (Recommended / Precise / Custom) ‚Äî if a preset is renamed or dropped, users persisted on the old value will see the same blank-render bug.
